title: New Wetland Attracts Wildlife at Great Fen
summary: A newly constructed wetland at the Great Fen nature reserve in Cambridgeshire is already attracting wildlife. The 25-hectare site, equivalent to 60 football pitches, took a year to build and includes a mere, channels, and reedbeds. Birds such as lapwings have been observed exploring the area for food. This project is part of the Peatland Progress initiative, funded by the National Lottery Heritage Fund, which aims to link two remaining fragments of ancient fen: Woodwalton Fen and Holme Fen. The Great Fen vision began in 2000, led by the Wildlife Trust for Bedfordshire, Cambridgeshire and Northamptonshire in partnership with other organizations. Project manager Lorna Parker noted that rainfall has naturally filled the water features, creating muddy edges ideal for insects and molluscs, which provide food for wading birds like lapwings, redshank, and snipe. The site is expected to open to the public in summer, allowing visitors to witness the land returning to nature for the first time in over a century.

questions.question: What are the two ancient fens being linked?

background_read: The Great Fen is a large wetland restoration project in Cambridgeshire, England. It aims to create a 3,700-hectare wetland landscape by linking two ancient fragments of fen. The project started in 2000 and involves multiple partners. Wetlands are important ecosystems that support biodiversity, store carbon, and reduce flood risk. The Peatland Progress project is funded by the National Lottery Heritage Fund and runs for five years.
Article_Structure: The article reports on a new wetland built at Great Fen. Main points include the size (25 hectares), construction time (one year), and early wildlife sightings (lapwings). The purpose is to inform about conservation progress. Evidence includes quotes from project manager Lorna Parker. Author credibility is high as BBC Science is a reputable source. The methodology involves on-site observation and expert commentary.

perspectives.description: The wetland is a key step in restoring ancient fenland and supporting biodiversity.
The site will offer educational and recreational opportunities for visitors.
